site.title: Dan's Radar
site.subtitle: A weekly signal feed on innovation, AI, frontier models, web dev, browsers, cybersecurity and agentic workflows — curated by Dan Berte.
site.author: Dan Berte

2026-07-10.tldr: A frontier lab published new agentic-coding eval results, changing the benchmark picture teams use when standardizing on a model for agent stacks. Chromium shipped a native agent-permission surface behind a flag, moving agent consent UX into the browser itself and making it the de facto security boundary for agentic browsing. Olivia Moore of a16z argues the AI-browser category is not dead but now needs a step-change feature to justify switching, since general chat agents becoming competent at browser use eroded its original value prop. An npm supply-chain compromise hit a popular build plugin — the third incident in that dependency class this quarter — and projects assembled with coding agents inherit these dependencies transitively, often without lockfile review.

entries.title: AI browsers need a 100x feature now that chat agents browse well
entries.author: Olivia Moore (@omooretweets)
entries.blurb: Olivia Moore (a16z) argues the AI-browser category is not dead, but switching costs are high enough that a 100x killer feature is now required: once agents inside ChatGPT and Claude got good at browser use, the category's original value prop eroded. A widely-seen investor read on where the bar for dedicated AI browsers now sits.

entries.title: Frontier lab publishes new agentic-coding eval results

entries.blurb: Why it matters: benchmark deltas like this shift which model teams standardize on for agent stacks; expect procurement questions within the quarter.

entries.title: Chromium ships native agent-permission surface behind a flag
entries.author: @example
entries.blurb: Why it matters: browsers becoming the OS for agents makes permission UX the new security boundary — directly relevant to IoT-style consent failures we've seen before.
